g.data-narration: Disk Management covers how the OS prepares, organizes, and maintains disk drives for data storage.
Before a disk can store data, the OS must initialize it through disk management tasks including formatting and boot setup.
g.text: Disk management prepares new disks for use by initializing structures that the OS
and applications need. Without these tasks, the OS cannot store data or even start up.

g.data-narration: Disk management has four main areas: low-level formatting, logical formatting, boot block management, and swap-space management.
g.text: Four Main Areas of Disk Management

g.data-narration: Low-level formatting creates sectors on the disk with header, data (512 or 4096 bytes), and error-correcting code for reliability.
g.text: Creates sectors the disk controller can read/write. Done at factory. Each sector has:
Header: sector number, cylinder info | ECC: detects/corrects errors

g.data-narration: Logical formatting creates file system structures like superblock, inode table, and root directory on each partition after partitioning.
g.text: OS writes file system data structures onto each partition:
Raw disk: partition without file system, used for swap or databases

g.data-narration: The boot sequence goes: power on → BIOS/UEFI → MBR → bootstrap → boot block → OS kernel loads and starts the system.
g.text: MBR is sector 0 of boot disk (512 bytes) | Boot block loads from active partition

g.data-narration: The MBR contains bootstrap code (446 bytes), partition table (64 bytes for 4 partitions), and magic number 0x55AA (2 bytes).
g.text: Total size: 512 bytes (first sector of boot disk)

g.data-narration: Bad blocks are defective sectors that can cause data loss. The OS must detect and handle them through various recovery techniques.
g.text: Disk sectors can become defective over time. Two handling approaches:
• Bad blocks marked in bad-block list
• Example: MS-DOS FORMAT marks bad FAT sectors
• Controller uses sector sparing or slipping

g.data-narration: Sector sparing remaps a bad sector to a spare sector from a pool. The OS sees the original logical sector but data goes to the spare.
g.text: Controller remaps bad sector to a spare sector transparently:

g.data-narration: Sector slipping shifts all subsequent sectors to fill the gap, preserving sequential access without seeking to a distant spare sector.
g.text: Instead of remapping to distant spare, shift adjacent sectors to fill the gap:

g.data-narration: Swap space extends RAM by using disk storage. When RAM is full, less-used pages are moved to swap to make room for new processes.
g.text: Disk space used as extension of main memory. When RAM is full, OS swaps less-used pages to disk:

g.data-narration: Swap can be a file in the filesystem (easy to manage, slower) or a raw partition (faster, fixed size). Modern systems use raw partitions.
g.text: Modern systems use raw partitions for performance. Linux: mkswap, swapon | Windows: pagefile.sys

g.data-narration: Key points: Low-level format creates sectors, logical format creates file system, MBR boots the system, sector sparing handles bad blocks.
g.text: Swap space: Raw partition (faster) vs file (easier) | Modern systems swap pages, not entire processes

g.data-narration: For exams: know formatting levels difference, draw boot sequence, explain sector sparing vs slipping, compare swap types.
